content_en:
Ikhlas does not develop spontaneously; it must be actively cultivated through deliberate practices, sustained self-examination, and the kind of spiritual companionship that encourages honest self-knowledge. In this concluding chapter, Ibn Rajab turns from the theoretical and diagnostic to the practical: how does the Muslim believer actually cultivate and deepen sincerity in their daily religious life?

The first and most essential practice is the examination of intention (muhasabat al-niyyah) before undertaking any significant act of worship or good deed. Islamic scholars have recommended pausing before acts of worship — even briefly — to ask oneself: Why am I doing this? Who am I doing this for? Is there any desire for recognition, status, or reward from people mixed into my intention? This examination does not need to produce certainty or paralyze action; its purpose is to bring the intention into consciousness so that it can be directed deliberately toward Allah. Ibn Rajab quotes the practice of the companions of the Prophet, who were known to pray: 'O Allah, I intend such-and-such — accept it from me and purify it.'

A related practice is the deliberate cultivation of hidden good deeds — acts of worship and charity performed in complete privacy, with no possibility of human observation. Night prayer, private dhikr, anonymous charity, hidden fasting, secret supplication — these practices are the gymnasium of ikhlas, because they train the heart to find its satisfaction in Allah's knowledge rather than in human recognition. The classical scholars considered that a person who has no hidden devotional life — no acts of worship that only Allah knows about — should seriously question the quality of their relationship with Allah.

Ibn Rajab discusses the importance of not announcing good deeds. While there are situations in which making good deeds known is appropriate — such as when it encourages others or fulfills a public obligation — the default disposition of the sincere Muslim is privacy. When one is tempted to mention a good deed one has done, to drop hints about one's night prayers or charitable giving, to maneuver conversations so that one's piety becomes visible, these impulses should be recognized as symptoms of the desire for human recognition and resisted.

The quality of one's private character is also a measure of sincerity. The person who is patient and kind when observed but irritable and selfish in private has revealed that their visible good character was performance rather than reality. Ikhlas demands that one's best behavior be directed toward the unseen Observer — that one treats one's household with more kindness than one shows to strangers, that one's honesty is most scrupulous when there are no witnesses, that one's patience is deepest when there is no audience to be impressed by it.

The role of dua in cultivating ikhlas deserves emphasis. Ibn Rajab notes that the Prophet taught specific supplications for protection from riya and from deeds performed for the sake of people. Regularly asking Allah to purify one's intentions is not a sign of doubt about one's sincerity but of mature spiritual self-knowledge. The believer who asks Allah for ikhlas acknowledges both their need and their trust in Allah's ability to grant what they cannot generate entirely through their own efforts.

Finally, ibn Rajab addresses the importance of remembering death and the Day of Judgment as a constant purifier of intentions. When one genuinely contemplates standing before Allah and presenting one's deeds, the question of whether those deeds were performed for Allah or for people becomes acutely urgent. Death strips away every audience except the One that ultimately matters. The believer who keeps this reality present — who lives, as the Prophet counseled, with abundant remembrance of death — will find that worldly motivations for worship gradually lose their pull, and the heart becomes increasingly free to worship Allah for Allah alone.
content_id:
Ikhlas tidak berkembang secara spontan; ia harus dibudidayakan secara aktif melalui praktik-praktik yang disengaja, pemeriksaan diri yang berkelanjutan, dan jenis persahabatan spiritual yang mendorong pengetahuan diri yang jujur. Dalam bab penutup ini, Ibnu Rajab beralih dari yang teoritis dan diagnostik ke yang praktis: bagaimana mukmin Muslim sebenarnya membangun dan memperdalam ketulusan dalam kehidupan keagamaan sehari-hari mereka?

Praktik pertama dan paling esensial adalah pemeriksaan niat (muhasabatun-niyyah) sebelum mengambil ibadah atau amal baik yang signifikan apa pun. Para ulama Islam telah merekomendasikan berhenti sejenak sebelum ibadah — bahkan sebentar saja — untuk bertanya pada diri sendiri: Mengapa aku melakukan ini? Untuk siapa aku melakukan ini? Apakah ada keinginan untuk pengakuan, status, atau pahala dari manusia yang bercampur dalam niatku? Pemeriksaan ini tidak perlu menghasilkan kepastian atau melumpuhkan tindakan; tujuannya adalah membawa niat ke dalam kesadaran sehingga dapat diarahkan secara sengaja kepada Allah. Ibnu Rajab mengutip praktik para Sahabat Nabi ﷺ, yang dikenal berdoa: 'Ya Allah, aku berniat untuk melakukan ini-itu — terimalah dariku dan purnakanlah.'

Praktik yang terkait adalah budidaya yang disengaja dari amal-amal baik yang tersembunyi — ibadah-ibadah dan sedekah-sedekah yang dilakukan dalam privasi sepenuhnya, tanpa kemungkinan pengamatan manusia. Shalat malam, dzikir privat, sedekah anonim, puasa yang tersembunyi, doa yang rahasia — praktik-praktik ini adalah gimnasium ikhlas, karena mereka melatih hati untuk menemukan kepuasannya dalam ilmu Allah bukan dalam pengakuan manusia. Para ulama klasik mempertimbangkan bahwa seseorang yang tidak memiliki kehidupan devosional yang tersembunyi — tidak ada ibadah yang hanya Allah mengetahuinya — sebaiknya mempertanyakan secara serius kualitas hubungannya dengan Allah.

Ibnu Rajab membahas pentingnya tidak mengumumkan amal-amal baik. Meskipun ada situasi-situasi di mana menampakkan amal baik adalah tepat — seperti ketika mendorong orang lain atau memenuhi kewajiban publik — disposisi bawaan Muslim yang tulus adalah privasi. Ketika seseorang tergoda untuk menyebut amal baik yang telah dilakukannya, untuk menjatuhkan petunjuk tentang shalat malam atau pemberian sedekahnya, untuk mengatur percakapan agar kesalehannya menjadi terlihat, impuls-impuls ini harus diakui sebagai gejala-gejala keinginan untuk pengakuan manusia dan dilawan.

Kualitas karakter privat seseorang juga merupakan ukuran ketulusan. Orang yang sabar dan baik ketika diamati tetapi mudah marah dan egois secara privat telah mengungkapkan bahwa karakter baiknya yang terlihat adalah pertunjukan bukan kenyataan. Ikhlas menuntut bahwa perilaku terbaik seseorang diarahkan kepada Sang Pengawas yang tidak terlihat — bahwa seseorang memperlakukan keluarganya dengan lebih banyak kebaikan daripada yang ditunjukkan kepada orang asing, bahwa kejujurannya paling cermat ketika tidak ada saksi, bahwa kesabarannya paling dalam ketika tidak ada penonton yang akan terkesan olehnya.

Peran doa dalam membangun ikhlas layak ditekankan. Ibnu Rajab mencatat bahwa Nabi ﷺ mengajarkan doa-doa khusus untuk perlindungan dari riya' dan dari amal-amal yang dilakukan demi manusia. Secara teratur meminta Allah untuk memurnikan niat seseorang bukan tanda keraguan tentang ketulusannya tetapi pengetahuan diri spiritual yang matang. Mukmin yang meminta ikhlas kepada Allah mengakui baik kebutuhannya maupun kepercayaannya kepada kemampuan Allah untuk memberikan apa yang tidak dapat dihasilkannya sepenuhnya melalui usahanya sendiri.

Akhirnya, Ibnu Rajab membahas pentingnya mengingat kematian dan Hari Kiamat sebagai pemurni niat yang konstan. Ketika seseorang benar-benar merenungkan berdiri di hadapan Allah dan mempersembahkan amal-amalnya, pertanyaan tentang apakah amal-amal tersebut dilakukan untuk Allah atau untuk manusia menjadi sangat mendesak. Kematian menanggalkan setiap penonton kecuali Yang pada akhirnya penting. Mukmin yang menjaga kenyataan ini selalu hadir — yang hidup, sebagaimana Nabi ﷺ menasihatkan, dengan mengingat kematian yang melimpah — akan mendapati bahwa motivasi-motivasi duniawi untuk beribadah secara bertahap kehilangan daya tariknya, dan hati menjadi semakin bebas untuk menyembah Allah semata-mata karena Allah.
content_ar: Ikhlas does not develop spontaneously; it must be actively cultivated through deliberate practices, sustained self-examination, and the kind of spiritual companionship that encourages honest self-العلم. In this concluding الباب, Ibn Rajab turns from the theoretical and diagnostic to the practical: how does the مسلم المؤمن actually cultivate and deepen sincerity in their daily religious الحياة? The أولاً and most الجوهري practice is the examination of intention (muhasabat al-niyyah) قبل undertaking any المهم act of worship or الخير deed. Islamic العلماء have recommended pausing قبل acts of worship — even briefly — to ask oneself: Why am I doing this? Who am I doing this for? Is there any desire for recognition, status, or الأجر from people mixed into my intention? This examination does not need to produce certainty or paralyze action; its purpose is to bring the intention into consciousness لذا that it can be directed deliberately toward الله. Ibn Rajab quotes the practice of the الصحابة of النبي ﷺ, who were known to pray: 'O الله, I intend such-and-such — accept it from me and purify it.' A related practice is the deliberate cultivation of hidden الخير deeds — acts of worship and الصدقة performed in complete privacy, with no possibility of human observation. Night الصلاة, private الذكر, anonymous الصدقة, hidden الصيام, secret الدعاء — these practices are the gymnasium of ikhlas, لأن they train the القلب to find its satisfaction in الله's العلم rather than in human recognition. The الكلاسيكي العلماء considered that a person who has no hidden devotional الحياة — no acts of worship that only الله knows about — should seriously question the quality of their relationship with الله. Ibn Rajab discusses the importance of not announcing الخير deeds. بينما there are situations in which making الخير deeds known is appropriate — مثل عندما it encourages others or fulfills a public الالتزام — the default disposition of the sincere مسلم is privacy. عندما one is tempted to mention a الخير deed one has done, to drop hints about one's night الصلوات or charitable giving, to maneuver conversations لذا that one's piety becomes visible, these impulses should be recognized as symptoms of the desire for human recognition and resisted. The quality of one's private الأخلاق is أيضاً a measure of sincerity. The person who is patient and kind عندما observed but irritable and selfish in private has revealed that their visible الخير الأخلاق was performance rather than reality. Ikhlas demands that one's best السلوك be directed toward the unseen Observer — that one treats one's household with more اللطف than one shows to strangers, that one's honesty is most scrupulous عندما there are no witnesses, that one's الصبر is deepest عندما there is no audience to be impressed by it.
